parts.prompt:
[Option A] In the period circa 1200 to 1450, the expansion of empires such as the Mongol Empire facilitated trade and
communication across Eurasia.
Develop an argument that evaluates the extent to which Mongol expansion affected the peoples of Eurasia
during this period.
parts.scoring_notes: Student selects ONE option. Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence (2), Analysis & Reasoning (2). Total: 6 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning about the extent to which Mongol expansion affected peoples of Eurasia (circa 1200–1450).

required_elements: Defensible claim addressing Mongol impact on Eurasian peoples
Line of reasoning that evaluates extent
alternatives.correct_example: The Mongol conquest of China and the Middle East resulted in cultural syncretism between the Mongols and conquered peoples.

required_elements: Thesis with analytic categories (economic, cultural, political)
alternatives.correct_example: The Mongol conquests helped connect China to the rest of Eurasia not only due to the increase of Silk Road trade, but also by creating opportunities for the exchange of religions and scientific knowledge.

wrong_examples: The Mongol conquests affected many things, including the environment — does not focus on the topic of the prompt.
The Mongol conquests affected many of the peoples of Eurasia during this time period — defensible claim but no line of reasoning.
The most significant effect of the Mongols was the spread of the Mongol language across Eurasia — not historically defensible.
common_traps: Writing a thesis that merely restates the prompt.
Listing effects without establishing a line of reasoning or categories.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Responds to the prompt with a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning. The thesis must make a claim that responds to the prompt, rather than restating or rephrasing the prompt, and must consist of one or more sentences located in one place, either in the introduction or the conclusion.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol conquest of China and the Middle East resulted in cultural syncretism between the Mongols and conquered peoples.
sample_responses.commentary: Establishes a line of reasoning that evaluates the topic (cultural syncretism as the mode of Mongol impact) — a historically defensible, specific claim.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol conquests helped connect China to the rest of Eurasia not only due to the increase of Silk Road trade, but also by creating opportunities for the exchange of religions and scientific knowledge.
sample_responses.commentary: Establishes a line of reasoning with analytic categories (trade, religion, scientific knowledge) — earns the thesis point.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol conquests destroyed many Eurasian cities.
sample_responses.commentary: Minimally acceptable thesis/claim — establishes a defensible line of reasoning, albeit brief.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol conquests affected many of the peoples of Eurasia during this time period.
sample_responses.commentary: Historically defensible but does not establish a line of reasoning — merely restates the prompt.

sample_responses.response: The most significant effect of the Mongols was the spread of the Mongol language across Eurasia.
sample_responses.commentary: Not historically defensible — Mongol language did not spread widely across Eurasia.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe a broader historical context relevant to Mongol expansion or the peoples of Eurasia.

required_elements: Broader context from before, during, or after the time frame
Must be more than a phrase or reference
alternatives.correct_example: The Mongol empire under Chinggis Khan developed in the context of a Jin dynasty that, while militarily powerful, was beginning to experience internal discord and a weak ruler.

required_elements: Context from another listed category (e.g., Silk Road pre-Mongol, Islamic/Christian/Buddhist spread, Song/Abbasid decline)
alternatives.correct_example: The importance of Silk Road trade to commerce and culture across Eurasia predated the Mongols — the Silk Road had already connected China to the Mediterranean through Central Asian intermediaries under the Tang and Abbasid empires, setting the stage for Mongol intensification of these networks.

wrong_examples: Many empires developed in Eurasia before the Mongols formed their own empire and these empires affected peoples across Eurasia — overgeneralized.
The Silk Roads linked many cultures across Eurasia — passing phrase without developed context.
Before the Mongols, there were many things happening — vague, not connected to the argument.
common_traps: Providing a phrase-level context (SG requires more than a passing phrase).
Contextualizing the whole world without connecting to the Mongol/Eurasia topic.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Describes a broader historical context relevant to the prompt. Context must relate the topic of the prompt to broader historical events, developments, or processes that occur before, during, or continue after the time frame of the question. To earn this point, the context provided must be more than a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol empire under Chinggis Khan developed in the context of a Jin dynasty that, while militarily powerful, was beginning to experience internal discord and a weak ruler.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes broader context (Jin dynasty weakening) and connects to the emergence of Mongol power — developed beyond a phrase.

sample_responses.response: The Mongols' nomadic background made them great warriors.
sample_responses.commentary: Minimally acceptable contextualization — brief, but describes the nomadic background context relevant to Mongol military success.

sample_responses.response: Many empires developed in Eurasia before the Mongols formed their own empire and these empires affected peoples across Eurasia.
sample_responses.commentary: Overgeneralized statement — does not describe a specific broader context or connect to the argument.

sample_responses.response: The Silk Roads linked many cultures across Eurasia.
sample_responses.commentary: Passing phrase — does not develop the context or connect to the argument.

scoring_points.description: Evidence (1 point): Identify at least two specific historical examples relevant to the Mongol conquests.

required_elements: Two specific historical examples relevant to the Mongol conquests
Examples must be specific (not vague generalizations)
alternatives.correct_example: The Mongol conquests led to the spread of technologies, such as gunpowder weapons, as well as knowledge such as Islamic science and math.

wrong_examples: Some Chinese learned the Mongol language in order to obtain high state positions — relevant but singular, and historically weak.
The Mongols traded — too vague, no specific named evidence.
Mongols were bad — no evidence of any kind.
common_traps: Specific evidence per SG: gunpowder, paper money, caravanserai, khanates, Chinggis/Khubilai Khan, Black Death, destroyed cities (Baghdad), Pax Mongolica, Yam post system, Mongol religious conversion/toleration, Song defeat.
Evidence must be at least two specific items — one specific example is insufficient.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Provides specific examples of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. To earn this point, the response must identify at least two specific historical examples relevant to the Mongol conquests.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol conquests led to the spread of technologies, such as gunpowder weapons, as well as knowledge such as Islamic science and math.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — provides two specific pieces of evidence (gunpowder diffusion + Islamic science/math transfer) relevant to Mongol impact.

sample_responses.response: The Mongols destroyed Baghdad in 1258 and ended the Abbasid Caliphate. They also established the Yam postal relay system that allowed communication across their vast empire.
sample_responses.commentary: Two specific named pieces of evidence (Baghdad 1258 / Yam system) drawn from the SG's list of acceptable specific examples.

sample_responses.response: Some Chinese learned the Mongol language in order to obtain high state positions.
sample_responses.commentary: Only one piece of evidence, and not specific or well-documented as a major effect of Mongol expansion.

scoring_points.description: Evidence (2nd point): Use at least two specific historical examples to support an argument about the extent to which Mongol expansion affected the peoples of Eurasia.

required_elements: Evidence explicitly supporting the argument about extent of Mongol impact
At least two specific items
alternatives.correct_example: Most of Europe did not experience much cultural change as a result of the Mongol conquests. Although the Mongols invaded Eastern Europe in 1241, their cultural impact was mostly limited to areas where nomadic influence was already strong.

required_elements: Evidence supporting an argument about Islamic world transformation
alternatives.correct_example: The Islamic world was substantially changed by the Mongol conquests. The Mongols conquered the Abbasid Caliphate, killed the caliph, and utterly destroyed many wealthy and powerful Eurasian trading cities.

wrong_examples: Listing evidence without connecting to an argument about extent.
Evidence is named but reasoning about extent is absent.
common_traps: Two pieces of evidence can come from the same sub-argument OR split across multi-part argument — either approach earns the point as long as both pieces support the thesis.
Must explicitly connect each piece of evidence to the evaluative claim about extent.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Supports an argument in response to the prompt using specific and relevant examples of evidence. To earn this point, the response must use at least two specific historical examples to support an argument regarding the extent to which Mongol expansion affected the peoples of Eurasia during the period circa 1200–1450.

sample_responses.response: Most of Europe did not experience much cultural change as a result of the Mongol conquests. Although the Mongols invaded Eastern Europe in 1241, their cultural impact was mostly limited to areas where nomadic influence was already strong.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — uses multiple specific pieces of evidence (1241 invasion, regional nomadic influence) to support the argument that Mongol cultural impact in Europe was limited.

sample_responses.response: The Islamic world was substantially changed by the Mongol conquests. The Mongols conquered the Abbasid Caliphate, killed the caliph, and utterly destroyed many wealthy and powerful Eurasian trading cities.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — uses multiple specific pieces of evidence (Abbasid conquest, killing of caliph, destruction of trading cities) to support the argument that the Islamic world was substantially transformed.

sample_responses.response: The Mongols did things.
sample_responses.commentary: No specific evidence and no argumentative connection.

scoring_points.description: Analysis & Reasoning (1 point): Use historical reasoning (comparison, causation, continuity and change) to frame or structure an argument about the extent to which Mongol expansion affected Eurasian peoples.

required_elements: Historical reasoning explicitly used to structure the argument
Applied to advance the claim, not just named
alternatives.correct_example: Overall, the Mongol empire had a positive economic effect because the Mongol conquests led to a considerable increase in trade across the Silk Roads.

wrong_examples: The Mongols destroyed Baghdad and killed the Abbasid caliph — evidence without reasoning connecting it to an argument.
Naming 'causation' without applying it to structure the argument.
Describing events chronologically without using causation, comparison, or CCOT.
common_traps: Listing evidence without reasoning — the SG stresses that evidence must be connected to an argument via historical reasoning.
Merely stating 'this caused that' without explaining the mechanism.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses historical reasoning (e.g., comparison, causation, continuity and change) to frame or structure an argument that addresses the prompt. The reasoning might be uneven, imbalanced, or not as effective as the most sophisticated responses, but it must be present to earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Overall, the Mongol empire had a positive economic effect because the Mongol conquests led to a considerable increase in trade across the Silk Roads.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — establishes an economic development caused by the Mongol conquests that affected peoples in Eurasia. Uses causation as the historical reasoning skill.

sample_responses.response: Explaining how the Mongol conquests affected peoples in Eurasia by fostering cultural exchange across Eurasia.
sample_responses.commentary: SG example of using causation/CCOT to frame an argument about Mongol cultural impact.

sample_responses.response: The Mongols destroyed Baghdad and killed the Abbasid caliph.
sample_responses.commentary: Evidence without reasoning — does not connect to an argument about extent of Mongol impact.

scoring_points.description: Analysis & Reasoning (2nd point): Demonstrate a complex understanding of the extent to which Mongol expansion affected the peoples of Eurasia.

required_elements: Sustained complex understanding — not a phrase or conclusion only

Ways to earn: nuance through multiple variables; similarity+difference / continuity+change / multiple causes / causes+effects; cross-period/cross-regional connections; corroboration of multiple perspectives; qualification by diverse views/evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Explaining the nuance of the issue by showing how the Mongol conquests caused enormous destruction but also led to long-term economic benefits for peoples in many Eurasian societies.

required_elements: Connecting Mongol effects to other instances of nomadic imperial expansion
alternatives.correct_example: Explaining relevant and insightful connections across time and space, such as by showing how the effects of the Mongol conquests differed from or were similar to the effects of previous or later instances of nomadic imperial expansion.

wrong_examples: In conclusion, the Mongols had both positive and negative effects — complexity stated in conclusion only.
The Mongols were complex — mere reference.
Listing multiple causes without showing how they interact.
common_traps: Complexity must be sustained throughout the essay, not a single closing sentence.
Best path: nuance (destruction AND long-term benefits); cross-period connections; qualification via cultural continuity (Turkic conversion to Islam continuing during Mongol rule).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Demonstrates a complex understanding of the historical development that is the focus of the prompt, using evidence to corroborate, qualify, or modify an argument that addresses the question. This demonstration of complex understanding must be part of the argument, not merely a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol conquests caused enormous destruction but also led to long-term economic benefits for peoples in many Eurasian societies.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains nuance, considers both causes and effects, and qualifies an argument about Mongol impact.

sample_responses.response: The effects of the Mongol conquests differed from or were similar to the effects of previous or later instances of nomadic imperial expansion.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains relevant and insightful connections across time and space (Mongols vs. other nomadic imperial expansions).

sample_responses.response: Although the Mongol conquests led to cultural changes in many societies, the conquests also reinforced ongoing cultural trends, such as the conversion of Turkic nomads to Islam.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — qualifies an argument by recognizing continuity (ongoing Turkic conversion) alongside change.

sample_responses.response: The Mongol conquests had similar economic effects in the Middle East and China.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — corroborates an argument across regions, considering both causes and effects.

parts.prompt:
[Option B] In the period circa 1450 to 1750, rulers of land-based empires, such as the Mughal, the Ottoman, and the
Safavid empires, used a variety of religious, political, and economic methods to legitimize and consolidate their
power.
Develop an argument that evaluates the extent to which a land-based empire successfully consolidated or
expanded its power during this period.
parts.scoring_notes: Student selects ONE option. Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence (2), Analysis & Reasoning (2). Total: 6 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible claim about the extent to which a land-based empire successfully consolidated or expanded its power during 1450–1750.

required_elements: Claim focused on at least one land-based empire (Ottoman, Safavid, Mughal, or Qing) consolidating/expanding power
Line of reasoning or analytic categories
alternatives.correct_example: The Safavids, Ottomans, and Mughals used monumental architecture and religious ideas to maintain and increase their power.

required_elements: Thesis with analytic categories (economic, military, religious)
alternatives.correct_example: Tax farming was used by Muslim states as a way to finance territorial expansion, build elaborate government and religious buildings, and promote state power.

wrong_examples: The Spanish expanded their empire by building colonies in the Americas — does not focus on a land-based empire of 1450–1750.
The rulers of these empires were extremely powerful — defensible but no line of reasoning.
The Mughals established a large group of khanates extending from China to Russia and the Middle East — not historically defensible.
common_traps: Writing about maritime empires instead of the land-based empires specified (Mughal, Ottoman, Safavid).
Listing methods without taking a position on extent of success.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Responds to the prompt with a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning. The thesis must make a claim that responds to the prompt, rather than restating or rephrasing the prompt, and must consist of one or more sentences located in one place, either in the introduction or the conclusion.

sample_responses.response: The Safavids, Ottomans, and Mughals used monumental architecture and religious ideas to maintain and increase their power.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — establishes a line of reasoning evaluating the topic (monumental architecture + religion as consolidation tools).

sample_responses.response: Tax farming was used by Muslim states as a way to finance territorial expansion, build elaborate government and religious buildings, and promote state power.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — establishes a line of reasoning with analytic categories (financing expansion, architectural projects, state power).

sample_responses.response: The Ottoman Empire used gunpowder weapons to expand.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — minimally acceptable thesis/claim establishing a defensible line of reasoning.

sample_responses.response: The Spanish expanded their empire by building colonies in the Americas.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not focus on the topic of the prompt (land-based empires; Spanish colonization was maritime expansion).

sample_responses.response: The rulers of these empires were extremely powerful.
sample_responses.commentary: Historically defensible claim but does not establish a line of reasoning.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe a broader historical context relevant to land-based empires consolidating or expanding power.

required_elements: Context from before, during, or after the time frame
More than a phrase or reference
alternatives.correct_example: The centralization of power within the Ottoman Empire can be understood as part of the broader practice of developing bureaucratic institutions in modernizing states across Eurasia during this period.

required_elements: Context tied to spread of Islam, Byzantine collapse, gunpowder technology, or Mongol successor states
alternatives.correct_example: Islam had spread to India long before the Mughal dynasty began expanding.

wrong_examples: The period from 1450 to 1750 was the golden age of Islamic land-based empires — overgeneralized, not broader context.
The great Muslim empires declined in importance and tried to modernize themselves through reforms — focuses on post-1750 developments, not broader context.
Passing reference without development.
common_traps: Context must be developed beyond a phrase.
Avoid context that is off-topic (e.g., American colonies).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Describes a broader historical context relevant to the prompt. Context must relate the topic of the prompt to broader historical events, developments, or processes. To earn this point, context provided must be more than a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: The centralization of power within the Ottoman Empire can be understood as part of the broader practice of developing bureaucratic institutions in modernizing states across Eurasia during this period.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — describes a broader bureaucratic-modernization trend in Eurasia and connects it to the Ottoman consolidation argument.

sample_responses.response: Islam had spread to India long before the Mughal dynasty began expanding.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — minimally acceptable contextualization. Establishes broader context (prior Islamic presence in India) relevant to Mughal consolidation.

sample_responses.response: The period from 1450 to 1750 was the golden age of Islamic land-based empires.
sample_responses.commentary: Overgeneralized statement about the time period — does not describe a specific broader context.

scoring_points.description: Evidence (1 point): Identify at least two specific historical examples relevant to land-based empires consolidating or expanding power.

required_elements: Two specific historical examples (named institutions, figures, policies)
Examples must relate to land-based empire consolidation/expansion
alternatives.correct_example: Mughal emperors used the zamindar tax collection system and built royal tombs such as the Taj Mahal.

wrong_examples: Portuguese entrance into the Indian Ocean and establishment of their trading post empire — wrong empire type (maritime, not land-based).
Mongols' allowance of religious tolerance and control of trade routes — outside the time period (pre-1450).
A vague statement about 'religion and the military' without specific examples.
common_traps: Specific evidence per SG: trained officials, standing armies, new military tactics, cannons, religious tolerance policies, Sunni-Shia rivalry, nobility-limiting policies, Taj Mahal, Ottoman tax farming, Mughal zamindars, Ottoman devshirme, janissaries, Suleymaniye Mosque, divine right theory, discriminatory taxes on minorities, Aztec tribute systems and monumental architecture.
Must name at least TWO specific items.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Provides specific examples of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. To earn this point, the response must identify at least two specific historical examples relevant to land-based empires consolidating or expanding power.

sample_responses.response: Mughal emperors used the zamindar tax collection system and built royal tombs such as the Taj Mahal.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — provides two specific pieces of evidence (zamindar system, Taj Mahal) relevant to Mughal consolidation.

sample_responses.response: The Ottomans used the devshirme system to recruit Christian boys into the Janissary corps, and they used gunpowder weapons to conquer Constantinople in 1453.
sample_responses.commentary: Two specific pieces of evidence (devshirme, Janissaries, gunpowder/Constantinople 1453) drawn from the SG's list of acceptable evidence.

sample_responses.response: Portuguese entrance into the Indian Ocean and the establishment of their trading post Empire.
sample_responses.commentary: Wrong empire type — the prompt asks about land-based empires, not maritime trading post empires.

scoring_points.description: Evidence (2nd point): Use at least two specific historical examples to support an argument about the extent to which a land-based empire successfully consolidated or expanded its power.

required_elements: Evidence connected to an argument about extent of consolidation/expansion
At least two specific items
alternatives.correct_example: The Ottomans developed the practice of using professionalized troops within standing armies as well as new gunpowder weapons in order to help them conquer new territories.

required_elements: Evidence for Mughal religious tolerance consolidation strategy
alternatives.correct_example: Some Mughal rulers practiced religious tolerance, for example by allowing Hindus to continue using their temples and exempting them from paying certain taxes. Such policies helped the Mughals establish their legitimacy.

wrong_examples: Listing evidence without connecting to the argument about extent.
Evidence that is accurate but not tied to consolidation/expansion.
common_traps: Must explicitly connect each piece of evidence to the consolidation/expansion argument.
Two pieces of evidence may be from the same or different empires, but must both support the thesis.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Supports an argument in response to the prompt using specific and relevant examples of evidence. To earn this point, the response must use at least two specific historical examples to support an argument regarding the extent to which a land-based empire successfully consolidated or expanded its power during the period circa 1450–1750.

sample_responses.response: The Ottomans developed the practice of using professionalized troops within standing armies as well as new gunpowder weapons in order to help them conquer new territories.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — uses multiple specific pieces of evidence (professional standing armies + gunpowder) to support the argument that the Ottomans expanded and consolidated territory.

sample_responses.response: Some Mughal rulers practiced religious tolerance, for example by allowing Hindus to continue using their temples and exempting them from paying certain taxes. Such policies helped the Mughals establish their legitimacy.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — uses multiple specific pieces of evidence (Hindu temples, jizya exemption) to support the argument that Mughal religious toleration consolidated power.

scoring_points.description: Analysis & Reasoning (1 point): Use historical reasoning (comparison, causation, CCOT) to frame or structure an argument about empire consolidation/expansion.

required_elements: Historical reasoning explicitly structuring the argument
Applied to advance the claim about extent of consolidation/expansion
alternatives.correct_example: Even though it preserved the power of the traditional Manchu nobility, the Qing dynasty also added elements of Chinese political culture based on Confucianism, which helped it achieve legitimacy and stability.

wrong_examples: Ottoman armies used cannons — no reasoning connecting evidence to argument.
Describing events without using causation, comparison, or CCOT.
Naming a reasoning skill without applying it.
common_traps: Evidence must be connected to argument via historical reasoning.
Best approaches per SG: explain centralizing administrative practices and their effects; or explain how changes in military organization/equipment led to territorial expansion.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses historical reasoning (e.g., comparison, causation, continuity and change) to frame or structure an argument that addresses the prompt. The reasoning might be uneven, imbalanced, or not as effective as the most sophisticated responses, but it must be present to earn this point.

sample_responses.response: Even though it preserved the power of the traditional Manchu nobility, the Qing dynasty also added elements of Chinese political culture based on Confucianism, which helped it achieve legitimacy and stability.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — uses causation to support an argument about Qing imperial administrative consolidation (preserving Manchu nobility + adding Confucian elements = legitimacy).

sample_responses.response: Explaining the effects of centralizing administrative practices that increased the stability and wealth of a land-based empire.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — SG-approved approach: centralization causation as structuring reasoning.

sample_responses.response: Ottoman armies used cannons.
sample_responses.commentary: Evidence without reasoning — does not structure an argument.

scoring_points.description: Analysis & Reasoning (2nd point): Demonstrate a complex understanding of land-based empire consolidation/expansion.

required_elements: Sustained complex understanding of extent of consolidation/expansion
Ways to earn: nuance via multiple variables; similarity/difference, continuity/change, causes, effects; cross-period/cross-regional connections; corroboration; qualification by diverse evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Explaining the nuance of an issue by analyzing multiple variables, such as explaining how the early Mughal emperors expanded their territory and centralized their administration, but the later Mughal emperors rapidly lost territory to a variety of indigenous elites as well as invaders.

required_elements: Connection across regions (e.g., similar processes among Turkish Muslim conquerors across Middle East, Persia, India)
alternatives.correct_example: Turkish Muslim conquerors established massive land-based empires in the Middle East, Persia, and India through similar processes of combining cavalry and gunpowder in their armies, as well as establishing centralized administrations.

wrong_examples: Complexity mentioned in the conclusion only.
Listing different empires without connecting them via a complex argument.
common_traps: Complexity must be sustained throughout the essay.

Best paths: Mughal early success + late fragmentation (nuance); Ottoman/Mughal tolerance vs. Safavid Shi'a enforcement (similarity/difference); Ottoman success + modernization failure (corroboration).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Demonstrates a complex understanding of the historical development that is the focus of the prompt, using evidence to corroborate, qualify, or modify an argument that addresses the question. This demonstration of complex understanding must be part of the argument, not merely a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: The early Mughal emperors expanded their territory and centralized their administration, but the later Mughal emperors rapidly lost territory to a variety of indigenous elites as well as invaders.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains nuance by analyzing multiple variables (early expansion vs. late fragmentation) and qualifies the argument.

sample_responses.response: Turkish Muslim conquerors established massive land-based empires in the Middle East, Persia, and India through similar processes of combining cavalry and gunpowder in their armies, as well as establishing centralized administrations.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains relevant and insightful connections across time and space (similar consolidation processes across three empires).

sample_responses.response: The Ottoman and Mughal Empires tolerated religious diversity in their populations, but the Safavid Empire supported only Shi'a Islamic practices in Persia.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains both similarities and differences (religious tolerance vs. Shi'a enforcement) across the three empires.

sample_responses.response: The Ottoman Empire successfully centralized its administration and competed against expanding European maritime empires but failed to modernize and eventually fell far behind European industrial economies.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — corroborates multiple perspectives (short-term consolidation success + long-term modernization failure).

parts.prompt:
[Option C] In the mid- to late twentieth century, a variety of political, military, and nonviolent methods were used to bring
about political and social change.
Develop an argument that evaluates the extent to which an individual, group, or movement in the mid- to late
twentieth century successfully challenged existing power structures during this period.
parts.scoring_notes: Student selects ONE option. Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence (2), Analysis & Reasoning (2). Total: 6 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible claim about the extent to which an individual, group, or movement in the mid- to late 20th century successfully challenged existing power structures.

required_elements: Names an individual, group, or movement
Claim about extent of success in challenging power structures
Line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: In the United States, Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. used peaceful civil disobedience to challenge the established racial hierarchies under segregation.

required_elements: Thesis with analytic categories (nonviolent + violent)
alternatives.correct_example: Anti-imperialist nationalist movements used non-violent as well as violent tactics to end colonialism and establish newly independent nations.

wrong_examples: The British Empire had territories around the globe — does not focus on challenges to power structures.
Muhammad Ali Jinnah was a successful attorney — historically defensible but no line of reasoning.
Gandhi fought a revolution to violently overthrow British rule in India — not historically defensible (Gandhi led a nonviolent movement).
common_traps: Thesis must evaluate extent — mere 'they succeeded' is insufficient.
Avoid non-defensible claims (misrepresenting Gandhi's tactics or Jinnah's role).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Responds to the prompt with a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning. The thesis must make a claim that responds to the prompt, rather than restating or rephrasing the prompt, and must consist of one or more sentences located in one place, either in the introduction or the conclusion.

sample_responses.response: In the United States, Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. used peaceful civil disobedience to challenge the established racial hierarchies under segregation.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — establishes a line of reasoning evaluating how MLK challenged racial power structures via nonviolent methods.

sample_responses.response: Anti-imperialist nationalist movements used non-violent as well as violent tactics to end colonialism and establish newly independent nations.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — establishes a line of reasoning with analytic categories (nonviolent vs. violent tactics).

sample_responses.response: Feminists worldwide protested against patriarchy.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — minimally acceptable thesis/claim establishing a defensible line of reasoning.

sample_responses.response: The British Empire had territories around the globe.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not focus on the topic of the prompt (challenging power structures).

sample_responses.response: Gandhi fought a revolution to violently overthrow British rule in India.
sample_responses.commentary: Not historically defensible — Gandhi led a nonviolent movement (satyagraha), not a violent revolution.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe a broader historical context relevant to challenges to power structures in the mid- to late 20th century.

required_elements: Context from before, during, or after the time frame
More than a phrase or reference
alternatives.correct_example: Movements that challenged power structures were often inspired by the revolutionary ideas of Karl Marx and socialism.

required_elements: Context tied to Cold War, decolonization, or rights-based movements
alternatives.correct_example: The Cold War forced many decolonization leaders to choose sides.

wrong_examples: Despite the two World Wars, existing power structures remained largely intact in the mid- to late twentieth century — not relevant as context for power challenges.
In the late twentieth century people used non-violent methods to bring about social change — overgeneralized, passing phrase.
Context provided but not connected to the argument.
common_traps: Context must be developed beyond a phrase and connected to the argument.

SG-approved contexts: partition of post-WWII states, Cold War, post-WWII violence, decolonization, socialism/communism, rights movements, NATO/Warsaw Pact, totalitarianism, second-wave feminism, UN Declaration of Rights, Western prosperity.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Describes a broader historical context relevant to the prompt. Context must relate the topic of the prompt to broader historical events, developments, or processes. To earn this point, context provided must be more than a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: Movements that challenged power structures were often inspired by the revolutionary ideas of Karl Marx and socialism.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — describes a broader intellectual context (Marxist/socialist thought) and connects it to the topic of power challenges.

sample_responses.response: The Cold War forced many decolonization leaders to choose sides.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — minimally acceptable contextualization. Describes the Cold War context and connects it to decolonization-era power challenges.

sample_responses.response: Despite the two World Wars, existing power structures remained largely intact in the mid- to late twentieth century.
sample_responses.commentary: Context provided but not relevant to the prompt topic of successful challenges to power structures.

scoring_points.description: Evidence (1 point): Identify at least two specific historical examples relevant to challenging power structures in the mid- to late 20th century.

required_elements: Two specific historical examples (named individuals, movements, or events)
Examples must relate to challenging power structures
alternatives.correct_example: Ho Chi Minh led the Vietnamese in fighting against French imperialism and Sukarno led the Indonesian struggle against the Dutch.

wrong_examples: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels' Communist Manifesto directly challenged the political power of the bourgeoisie — relevant but OUTSIDE the time period (1848).
'Antiwar protests spread around the world during the 1970s' — too vague, no named groups or events.
Reference to mid-20th-century events without specificity.
common_traps: SG acceptable evidence: Gandhi/Nkrumah, Indian National Congress, salt marches/satyagraha, MLK/Mandela, US Civil Rights (boycotts, sit-ins, March on Washington), ANC, Shining Path/Viet Minh, Al-Qaeda, IRA/Tamil Tigers, proxy wars, 68ers/Second Wave Feminists, Greenpeace.
Evidence must be within mid- to late-20th-century time period.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Provides specific examples of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. To earn this point, the response must identify at least two specific historical examples relevant to the challenging of power structures.

sample_responses.response: Ho Chi Minh led the Vietnamese in fighting against French imperialism and Sukarno led the Indonesian struggle against the Dutch.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — provides two specific named leaders and their anti-colonial challenges to European power structures.

sample_responses.response: Martin Luther King Jr. led nonviolent protests including the 1963 March on Washington, while Nelson Mandela and the African National Congress challenged South African apartheid.
sample_responses.commentary: Two specific pieces of evidence drawn from the SG's approved list (MLK + Mandela/ANC).

sample_responses.response: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels' Communist Manifesto directly challenged the political power of the bourgeoisie.
sample_responses.commentary: Outside the time period — the Communist Manifesto (1848) is not from the mid- to late 20th century.

scoring_points.description: Evidence (2nd point): Use at least two specific historical examples to support an argument about the extent to which an individual, group, or movement challenged existing power structures.

required_elements: Evidence connected to an argument about extent of success
At least two specific items
alternatives.correct_example: The Indian nationalist movement led by Mohandas Gandhi succeeded at ousting the British Empire through dedication to the principle of nonviolent resistance as witnessed by his marches and protests.

required_elements: Evidence for environmentalist challenges to corporate power
alternatives.correct_example: During the late 20th century a variety of environmentalist groups like Green Peace tried to limit multinational corporations' activities in order to stop pollution and climate change. Some groups used peaceful methods while others turned to violence.

wrong_examples: Listing evidence without connecting to the argument.
Evidence accurate but not tied to an argument about extent of success.
common_traps: Evidence may come from a single movement OR split across multi-part argument.
Must connect each piece of evidence to the extent-of-success claim.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Supports an argument in response to the prompt using specific and relevant examples of evidence. To earn this point, the response must use at least two specific historical examples to support an argument regarding the extent which an individual, group, or movement in the mid- to late-twentieth century successfully challenged existing power structures.

sample_responses.response: The Indian nationalist movement led by Mohandas Gandhi succeeded at ousting the British Empire through dedication to the principle of nonviolent resistance as witnessed by his marches and protests.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — uses multiple specific pieces of evidence (Indian nationalism, nonviolent resistance, marches/protests) to support the argument that Indian nationalism successfully challenged British imperialism.

sample_responses.response: During the late 20th century a variety of environmentalist groups like Green Peace tried to limit multinational corporations' activities in order to stop pollution and climate change. Some groups used peaceful methods while others turned to violence.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — uses multiple specific pieces of evidence (Greenpeace, peaceful + violent methods) to support the argument that environmentalists opposed multinational corporations.

scoring_points.description: Analysis & Reasoning (1 point): Use historical reasoning (comparison, causation, CCOT) to frame or structure an argument about challenges to power structures.

required_elements: Historical reasoning explicitly structuring the argument
Applied to advance the claim about extent of success
alternatives.correct_example: The African National Congress protested the apartheid policies of the South African government and eventually gathered enough international support to bring about full democratic participation in South Africa.

wrong_examples: Antiwar protests spread around the world during the 1970s — evidence without reasoning.
Naming a reasoning skill without applying it.
Describing events without explaining causation, comparison, or continuity/change.
common_traps: Evidence without reasoning.
Best approaches per SG: explain how colonized peoples organized and resisted imperial rule; or explain effects of popular movements for democratic reform in Eastern Europe.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses historical reasoning (e.g., comparison, causation, continuity and change) to frame or structure an argument that addresses the prompt. The reasoning might be uneven, imbalanced, or not as effective as the most sophisticated responses, but it must be present to earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The African National Congress protested the apartheid policies of the South African government and eventually gathered enough international support to bring about full democratic participation in South Africa.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — uses causation to support an argument about ANC protests against apartheid leading to democratic participation.

sample_responses.response: Explaining how colonized peoples in Africa or Asia organized themselves and resisted European imperial rule.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — SG-approved approach using causation to structure the argument.

sample_responses.response: Antiwar protests spread around the world during the 1970s.
sample_responses.commentary: Evidence without reasoning — does not structure an argument.

scoring_points.description: Analysis & Reasoning (2nd point): Demonstrate a complex understanding of the extent to which an individual, group, or movement successfully challenged existing power structures.

required_elements: Sustained complex understanding of extent of success
Ways to earn: nuance via multiple variables; similarity/difference, continuity/change, causes, effects; cross-period/cross-regional connections; corroboration; qualification by diverse evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Between 1989 and 1992, in much of the world, anticommunist protests succeeded at overturning the Soviet Union, but in Asia, communist one-party states maintained their grip on power.

required_elements: Connection across regions showing common socialist-resistance tactics
alternatives.correct_example: Demonstrating the influence of socialist ideas and tactics in resisting power structures in Asia (Vietnam), Africa (Tanzania), as well as Latin America (Cuba).

wrong_examples: Complexity mentioned in conclusion only.
Listing movements without showing how they relate complexly.
common_traps: Complexity must be sustained throughout the essay.

Best paths per SG: partial success of anticommunist protests 1989-1992 (nuance/qualification); global influence of socialist resistance tactics (cross-regional connections); Mandela vs. Mugabe similar goals/different methods (similarity/difference); Western support for communism OR socialist allies of the West (corroboration of complexity).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Demonstrates a complex understanding of the historical development that is the focus of the prompt, using evidence to corroborate, qualify, or modify an argument that addresses the question. This demonstration of complex understanding must be part of the argument, not merely a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: Between 1989 and 1992, in much of the world, anticommunist protests succeeded at overturning the Soviet Union, but in Asia, communist one-party states maintained their grip on power.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains nuance and qualifies an argument by analyzing multiple variables (Soviet collapse vs. Asian communist persistence).

sample_responses.response: Demonstrating the influence of socialist ideas and tactics in resisting power structures in Asia (Vietnam), Africa (Tanzania), as well as Latin America (Cuba).
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains relevant and insightful connections across time and space (socialist tactics across three continents).

sample_responses.response: Comparing Nelson Mandela in South Africa with Robert Mugabe in Zimbabwe shows that similar groups with similar nationalist goals could use very different methods to achieve their goals.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains both similarities (nationalist goals) and differences (methods) to complicate the extent-of-success argument.

sample_responses.response: Evidence that some Westerners supported communism, or that some socialist independence leaders and movements chose to ally with the West, rather than with the Soviet bloc.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — corroborates multiple perspectives by exploring complexities of Cold War alignment during power-challenge movements.
